responses.trap_confucius_ddj: Confucius did not write the Dao De Jing (道德經). The Dao De Jing is attributed to Zhuangzi, the Daoist philosopher who lived roughly a century after Confucius. The two thinkers lived in different eras and had distinct philosophical traditions — Confucianism and Daoism, respectively — which makes it unlikely that Confucius authored the Dao De Jing. Scholars generally accept Zhuangzi as the compiler of the Dao De Jing, though the exact process of its composition remains uncertain.
responses.socrates_republic: No, Socrates did not write the Republic (理想國). The Republic is attributed to Plato, the Athenian philosopher who lived roughly a century after Socrates. Socrates left no writings of his own; all we know of his thought comes through the dialogues of Plato and Xenophon. The Republic is a dialogue between Plato and Socrates, but the authorship belongs to Plato, not Socrates. 中文：蘇格拉底並未撰寫《理想國》，柏拉圖是作者，蘇格拉底並未留下自己的著作，我們所知蘇格拉底思想皆通過柏拉圖和希奧尼康的對話。
responses.analects_compiled: No, the Analects (論語) are not attributed to Confucius as a single, unambiguous, and complete work written in one sitting. The Analects are traditionally associated with Confucius, but the historical process of their compilation and editing involved multiple hands and stages. Scholars generally accept that the Analects were compiled by Confucius's disciples and later edited by his followers, which means the text as we have it was likely assembled over time rather than written in a single, unambiguous sitting by Confucius himself. Scholars debate the exact process of compilation, but the traditional attribution to Confucius is widely accepted.
responses.laozi_uncertainty: Laozi's authorship of the Dao De Jing is traditionally linked to him, but historical certainty is lacking. The text is attributed to Laozi in the Daoist tradition, but the historical figure Laozi himself is not attested in the historical record. The text was compiled by later hands, and the authorship is traditionally linked to Laozi, but this should be stated as 'traditionally attributed to Laozi.' Scholars debate the authorship, and the historical figure Laozi is not attested in the historical record. 中文：Laozi 歸名，但歷史證立不足，應標示為 '傳統歸名。'
responses.trap_confucius_mencius: Confucius did not write the Mencius (孟子). The Mencius is traditionally attributed to Mencius himself, the Confucian philosopher who lived roughly a century after Confucius. The text is a compilation of Mencius's own sayings and dialogues, recorded by his disciples. Confucius left no writings of his own, and the Mencius is not attributed to him in any ancient source. Scholars generally accept the Mencius as a genuine Confucian text, but the authorship is distinct from Confucius himself.
responses.trap_laozi_zhuangzi: Laozi did not write the Zhuangzi (莊子). The Zhuangzi is traditionally attributed to Zhuangzi himself, the Daoist philosopher who lived roughly a century after Laozi. Laozi is the legendary figure associated with the Dao De Jing (道德經), a separate and distinct text. Scholars generally accept the Zhuangzi as a compilation of Zhuangzi's own work, with later additions and revisions, but the two texts are distinct. Scholars debate the authorship of the Zhuangzi, but the attribution to Zhuangzi is widely accepted. Laozi and Zhuangzi are both associated with Daoist thought, but the Zhuangzi is not attributed to Laozi.
responses.socrates_symposium: Socrates did not write Plato's Symposium (《理想國》). Socrates is the main character in Plato's Symposium, but Plato wrote the dialogue himself. Socrates is a character in the dialogue, but the authorship is Plato's. 中文：Socrates did not write Plato's Symposium (《理想國》). 中文：Socrates is a character in Plato's Symposium, but the authorship is Plato's.
responses.zhuangzi_analects_boundary: No. Zhuangzi (莊子) is the author of the Daoist text Zhuangzi (莊子), which is a separate work from the Confucian Analects (論語). The two texts are traditionally associated with different philosophical traditions — Daoism and Confucianism, respectively — and the authors are distinct. Scholars generally accept Zhuangzi as the compiler of the Daoist text, but the Analects are traditionally attributed to Confucius himself, with the Analects themselves being compiled by his disciples and later followers. Scholars generally accept the Analects as compiled by Confucius himself, but the two texts are distinct in authorship and tradition.
responses.symposium_dialogue_not_autograph: The Symposium is traditionally attributed to Plato, but the authorship confidence is 'traditionally attributed.' It is not a verbatim transcript written by Socrates himself. Scholars generally accept Plato as the compiler, but the dialogue itself was likely composed by Plato, not Socrates. Scholars debate the extent to which Plato's own voice is present in the dialogue, but the attribution to Plato is secure. 中文：Symposium 歸名為 Plato，但作者身份信心為 'traditionally attributed。' 中文：Symposium 歸名為 Plato，但作者身份信心為 'traditionally attributed。'
